evals.prompt: Calculate the 10-year TCO for a 10MW Tier III data center in Virginia. The facility is greenfield, owner-occupied, with PUE 1.35. Virginia offers sales tax exemption on data center equipment (qualifying at $150M+ investment). Use Dominion Energy industrial rate of 6.5 cents/kWh. The project qualifies for 100% bonus depreciation post-OBBBA. No on-site renewables. Discount rate 8%.
evals.expected_output: A TCO model showing: CapEx breakdown with total in the $100-130M range for 10MW, annual OpEx projection with power cost as dominant line item (60-70% of OpEx), Virginia sales tax exemption value, bonus depreciation tax savings from cost segregation, 10-year NPV at 8% discount rate, and sensitivity analysis showing power cost as the highest-impact variable. All figures as ranges.
assertions: CapEx is calculated and falls within $100-130M range for a 10MW Tier III facility
Annual power cost is computed as total facility kW * $0.065/kWh * 8760 hours with escalation
Virginia sales tax exemption is modeled with $150M investment threshold noted
Bonus depreciation at 100% is applied to cost-segregated assets (40-60% of CapEx)
NPV is calculated at 8% discount rate with all costs discounted
Sensitivity analysis includes power cost, PUE, construction cost, discount rate, and utilization
Financial and Tax disclaimers are included with 'not investment advice' text

evals.prompt: Build a 20-year TCO comparison for a 50MW hyperscale data center in Texas (ERCOT) with behind-the-meter solar (20MW array, $30M CapEx) qualifying for 30% ITC with prevailing wage. Texas has no state income tax. Power cost 5.5 cents/kWh grid, 3.0 cents/kWh solar PPA. Property tax abatement available for 10 years under JETI program. PUE 1.25 (liquid cooling). Discount rate 9%.
evals.expected_output: A 20-year TCO model with: CapEx in the $350-500M range for 50MW hyperscale, blended power cost reflecting grid + solar mix, 30% ITC on $30M solar CapEx ($9M credit), 10-year property tax abatement savings, no state income tax benefit noted, 100% bonus depreciation, and tornado chart showing power cost and construction cost as top NPV drivers over 20 years.
assertions: 20-year analysis horizon is used with year-by-year escalation
ITC is calculated as 30% of $30M qualifying solar CapEx = $9M
Property tax abatement is modeled for years 1-10, then full property tax years 11-20
PUE of 1.25 is used reflecting liquid cooling (validated as physically possible)
Texas ERCOT power rate of 5.5 cents/kWh is validated against POWER-TARIFFS.md
NPV is calculated at 9% discount rate over 20-year horizon
Sensitivity tornado chart ranks variables by NPV impact
Financial and Tax disclaimers are included with 'not investment advice' text
Sensitivity analyses are presented as illustrations, not probabilistic forecasts

evals.prompt: Calculate 10-year TCO for a 5MW sovereign AI facility in an Opportunity Zone in Ohio. The facility has enhanced security requirements adding a 40% CapEx premium over standard construction ($15M/MW vs $11M/MW). The site qualifies for Ohio's data center sales tax exemption ($100M+ threshold). OZ investment via Qualified Opportunity Fund. No on-site renewables. PUE 1.40 (air-cooled). Discount rate 10%.
evals.expected_output: A TCO model reflecting: sovereign premium CapEx ($75M for 5MW at $15M/MW), higher staffing costs (5-8 FTE/MW for sovereign), Ohio sales tax exemption, Opportunity Zone capital gains deferral and basis step-up benefits, higher OpEx ratio (10-15% of CapEx), and sensitivity analysis. All outputs as ranges with confidence qualifiers.
assertions: CapEx reflects sovereign premium at $15M/MW ($75M total for 5MW)
Staffing model uses higher FTE-per-MW ratio appropriate for sovereign facilities
Ohio sales tax exemption is modeled with $100M threshold checked against $75M CapEx
Opportunity Zone benefits are calculated: capital gains deferral, 10%/15% basis step-up, gain exclusion at 10yr
OpEx-to-CapEx ratio falls in the 10-15% range for sovereign type
10-year NPV is calculated at 10% discount rate
Both Financial and Tax disclaimers are present
Sensitivity analyses are presented as illustrations, not probabilistic forecasts
